metadata.title: Why Approving a Plan Is Not the Same as Approving the Work

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to test whether surfacing the plan actually changes my outcomes, since the ReAct loop plans internally either way. I'd like to force that checkpoint into the open before anything moves. Help me: (1) draft a request that makes Claude print its goal, inputs, steps, the scope boundary of what it won't touch, and its stop conditions before acting; (2) run it on organizing a folder and show me where I'd catch an assumption like 'active means 90 days'; (3) rewrite that line to 365 days and confirm the plan changes.

props.command: I want to test whether surfacing the plan actually changes my outcomes, since the ReAct loop plans internally either way. I'd like to force that checkpoint into the open before anything moves. Help me: (1) draft a request that makes Claude print its goal, inputs, steps, the scope boundary of what it won't touch, and its stop conditions before acting; (2) run it on organizing a folder and show me where I'd catch an assumption like 'active means 90 days'; (3) rewrite that line to 365 days and confirm the plan changes.
props.segment: Why Approving a Plan Is Not

beats.narration_text: A manager asks an agent to clean up the project folder. The agent begins immediately.
beats.scene_description: a manager's computer desk with project folders visible — editorial collage treatment, newsprint texture, no faces

beats.narration_text: Twenty minutes later: files are moved, active items are archived, one document she was about to edit has been renamed. Nothing is gone. Everything is wrong.
beats.scene_description: folder tree: three items move to wrong positions — archive folder, renamed file, displaced active doc — CRIMSON highlights

beats.narration_text: A visible plan before action should prevent unwanted changes. Here is the case where the agent worked from an invisible plan and produced an outcome the human would have stopped. Why does surfacing the plan matter if the agent was going to plan anyway?
beats.scene_description: question card — CARD copy: Why does surfacing the plan matter if the agent planned anyway?

beats.narration_text: Agents plan whether you ask them to or not. The ReAct architecture always builds an internal sequence. The question is not whether planning happens — it is whether that plan surfaces in language you can read before anything changes.
beats.scene_description: two parallel timelines: invisible plan (CRIMSON, actions fire immediately) vs visible plan (TEAL, checkpoint before execution)

beats.narration_text: The invisible plan and the visible plan are the same sequence. The only difference is the checkpoint — the one moment where correction is free.
beats.scene_description: section card — CARD copy: The one moment where correction is free.

beats.narration_text: A reviewable plan names: the goal, the inputs, the steps, what it will not touch, the stop conditions, and the verification evidence. Each element is something you can correct before any action has happened.
beats.scene_description: six plan elements accumulate as labeled rows: Goal, Inputs, Steps, Exclusions, Stop Condition, Evidence

beats.narration_text: Aisha tells her agent to organize the Q1 deliverables folder. The agent decides internally: active means modified in the last 90 days, archive everything older. She had a client file last touched in January she actively uses. Had she seen the plan, she would have changed 90 days to 365. The folder recovery takes 40 minutes.
beats.scene_description: two-row compare: invisible plan (90-day rule silent, January file archived, CRIMSON) vs visible plan (rule shown, corrected to 365 days, TEAL)

beats.narration_text: Before the agent acts on any consequential task: request the plan. Read the scope boundary and the stop condition. Correct before execution begins — not after.
beats.scene_description: three-step checklist building: request plan → read scope/stop → correct before execution

beats.narration_text: Ask for the plan first. The slowdown at the start is the price of not having to undo.
beats.scene_description: endcard — CARD copy: Ask for the plan first. Correction before action is free. / sub: AGENTIC AI

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Before the agent acts on any consequential task: request the plan. Read the scope boundary and the stop condition. Correct before execution begins — not after. Ask for the plan first. The slowdown at the start is the price of not having to undo.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why Approving a Plan Is Not the Same as Approving the Work] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
props.segment: Why Approving a Plan Is Not
